coredata.dc:description: The present study is aimed to develop the nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) based nanocarrier to target epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) in the treatment of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). The N-GQDs were functionalized with GE11 peptide (a targeting agent) with high affinity for EGFR (GE11-N-GQDs), using carbodiimide chemistry. The studies revealed that the particle size of the N-GQDs increased to 96.88 ± 2.16 nm after NE loading (NE-N-GQDs). The GE11 functionalization and NE loading (GE11-NE-N-GQDs) further increased the particle size to 124.0 ± 1.76 nm. The particle shape of the GE11-NE-N-GQDs were cubic and quasi-hexagonal. The reduction in the crystallinity was evident by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. The GE11-NE-N-GQDs showed higher drug loading (97.42 ± 1.15 %) and entrapment efficiency (98.14 ± 1.32 %) than the NE-N-GQDs (97.38 ± 1.46 %, 92.36 ± 1.68 %). The N-GQDs based nanocarriers displayed good antioxidant activity and hemocompatibility. The GE11-NE-N-GQDs displayed pH dependent and prolonged release (99.78 ± 2.20 %) at 50 h. The %cell viability of the GE11-NE-N-GQDs treated A549 (human lung adenocarcinoma) cells was less (IC50: 1 µg/ml) than the NE-N-GQDs (IC50: 3 µg/ml) and free NE (IC50: 6.60 µg/ml). The GE11-N-GQDs enhanced intracellular uptake and arrested G1 phase which increased apoptosis and number of cells in sub-G1 phase. Hence, the results showed the GE11-N-GQDs as a promising approach to target EGFR overexpression in NSCLC.

head.ce:title: Peptide conjugated nintedanib loaded graphene quantum dots: Characterization and cell based studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study is aimed to develop the nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) based nanocarrier to target epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) in the treatment of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). The N-GQDs were functionalized with GE11 peptide (a targeting agent) with high affinity for EGFR (GE11-N-GQDs), using carbodiimide chemistry. The studies revealed that the particle size of the N-GQDs increased to 96.88 ± 2.16 nm after NE loading (NE-N-GQDs). The GE11 functionalization and NE loading (GE11-NE-N-GQDs) further increased the particle size to 124.0 ± 1.76 nm. The particle shape of the GE11-NE-N-GQDs were cubic and quasi-hexagonal. The reduction in the crystallinity was evident by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. The GE11-NE-N-GQDs showed higher drug loading (97.42 ± 1.15 %) and entrapment efficiency (98.14 ± 1.32 %) than the NE-N-GQDs (97.38 ± 1.46 %, 92.36 ± 1.68 %). The N-GQDs based nanocarriers displayed good antioxidant activity and hemocompatibility. The GE11-NE-N-GQDs displayed pH dependent and prolonged release (99.78 ± 2.20 %) at 50 h. The %cell viability of the GE11-NE-N-GQDs treated A549 (human lung adenocarcinoma) cells was less (IC50: 1 µg/ml) than the NE-N-GQDs (IC50: 3 µg/ml) and free NE (IC50: 6.60 µg/ml). The GE11-N-GQDs enhanced intracellular uptake and arrested G1 phase which increased apoptosis and number of cells in sub-G1 phase. Hence, the results showed the GE11-N-GQDs as a promising approach to target EGFR overexpression in NSCLC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is a prevalent type of lung cancer with the estimate of 85 % cases which account for majority of cancer related deaths [1]. The primary treatment for stage I or II NSCLC involves surgical removal of the tumor followed by adjuvant therapy. However, as the disease progresses to stage III or IV, the treatment approach is shifted to chemotherapy or radiotherapy. The surgical removal is considered as effective, but it possesses several limitations such as incomplete removal which often leads to recurrence of tumor. The surgery can cause pain, infection and loss of function in the affected area. Also, the surgery is not suitable for patients with poor health history. These limitations highlight the need for a comprehensive treatment plan that may include other therapies such as chemotherapy or radiation [2]. The selection of drug for the treatment becomes crucial as most of the patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage and disease progression needs to be managed at this stage in order to reduce mortality. These chemotherapeutics do not only affect cancer cells but also harm healthy cells which lead to numerous adverse effects [3,4]. Therefore, the efforts are directed towards developing targeted cancer therapies which mitigate the adverse reactions induced by chemotherapeutics while increasing anticancer effect [5,6].

A colloidal system like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have potential for cancer treatment by targeted drug delivery and radiation therapy. Their exceptional properties including high photoluminescence, high surface area, high colloidal stability, excellent biocompatibility, efficient photothermal conversion and surface functionalization capabilities make them appealing candidates for targeted cancer treatment and bioimaging. The surface functional groups of GQDs also allow them to be used as photosensitizer and generate reactive oxygen species upon exposure to specific wavelength of light and induce cancer cell death [7,8]. Their unique physiochemical properties naturally lead them to accumulate in the cell nucleus and interact with DNA. Consequently, the GQDs can induce oxidative DNA damage and thus, the effect of anticancer drugs is enhanced with GQDs [9]. Moreover, the flat structure of GQDs is filled with delocalized electrons which provides high capacity for loading of hydrophobic drugs via π-π stacking [10]. The nitrogen (N) doping can increase their specific surface area by reducing the size. Additionally, the N doping increases the number of aromatic rings by eliminating large number of defects which can increase the loading of hydrophobic drugs [11]. Moreover, the surface functional groups of the N doped GQDs (N-GQDs) can be easily modified with a targeting agent.

The targeted therapy utilizes tyrosine kinase inhibitors particularly when oncogene mutations are found in cancer cells. The overexpression of epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) has been identified with > 60 % of NSCLC cases [12] and has largely influenced lung cancer treatment. The EGFR plays vital role in regulating protein transcription, proliferation and cell differentiation. The dysregulation of EGFR is closely associated with oncogenesis and cancer progression. Given that activating EGFR mutation is the most prevalent in NSCLC and inhibiting EGFR represents an appealing therapeutic strategy [13]. The nintedanib esylate (NE) is a triple tyrosine kinase inhibitor which prevents angiogenesis and metastasis by blocking EGFR activity. The clinical trials indicates the use of NE for the effective treatment of NSCLC [14]. However, severe adverse reactions like abdominal pain, elevation of liver enzyme, weight loss and hypertension are reported with the conventional dosage form which indicate the need for an alternative approach such as nanoformulation preparation with targeting moieties [15]. Peptides are preferred targeting agent because they are easy to synthesize, small in size and have low immunogenicity. They can also penetrate tumors effectively [16]. The dodecapeptide (GE11) has been recognised as a promising targeting agent for EGFR overexpressed cancer cells owing to its high affinity for EGFR [17].

Hence, this research work was aimed to develop EGFR targeted N-GQDs by GE11 functionalization which would enhance internalization of the nanocarrier to increase the effect of NE. For this purpose, the prepared N-GQDs were functionalized with GE11 peptide using carbodiimide chemistry ( Fig. 1). The characteristics of GE11 functionalized NE loaded N-GQDs (GE11-NE-N-GQDs) were compared with the NE loaded N-GQDs (NE-N-GQDs). The efficiency of the developed formulation was proved by performing intracellular internalization, cell cytotoxicity and apoptosis studies.

The NE was offered as a gift sample from Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Ltd., India. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC.HCl) was purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL) Pvt. Ltd., India. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was purchased from Himedia, India. Triton-X-100 and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were purchased from Dual Life Sciences, India. GE11 peptide was purchased from NovoPro Bioscience Inc., China. Deionized water (solvent) was purchased from Labdhi Enterprises, India. The other chemicals and materials of analytical grade were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the NE loaded N-GQDs (NE-N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs were prepared and characterized as per the method described in our previous research [11]. The varying amount of NE was mixed with the N-GQDs (0.02 %, 200 µg/ml) to prepare solution mixtures with different N-GQDs:NE ratio (1:1–1:9) and stirred at 500 rpm for 24 h at room temperature. The obtained dispersion was dialyzed for 8 h to remove unbound NE. The fresh media was replaced at an interval of 2 h [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the GE11 functionalized N-GQDs (GE11-N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs were functionalized with GE11 peptide by carbodiimide method. The solution of EDC (0.1 %) and NHS (0.1 %) was prepared using water as a solvent (10 ml). The prepared solution mixture was stirred for 30 min under continuous stirring at room temperature to ensure the solubilisation of the reagents. Then, the solution mixture of the EDC and NHS was added in the aqueous solution of N-GQDs (0.5 %, 10 ml) to activate the carboxyl functional group (-COOH) and for stabilization of an intermediate product formed by the reaction between –COOH and EDC. The solution mixture was stirred for 6 h at room temperature. Following this, the aqueous solution of GE11 peptide (0.1 %, 2 ml) was drop-wise added into the above solution mixture under continuous stirring at 750 rpm and kept overnight for stirring at room temperature [19]. This step involves conjugation of the amine group (-NH2) introduced by the GE11 peptide with the activated –COOH functional group of the N-GQDs to form an amide (-CONH-) bond. After completion of the reaction, the resulting solution was dialyzed using water for 8 h to remove the excess amount of EDC and NHS. The media was replaced every 2 h. The final product was collected after dialysis and stored at 4°C until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the NE loaded GE11-N-GQDs (GE11-NE-N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The NE was mixed with the GE11-N-GQDs at different GE11-N-GQDs:NE ratio (1:1–1:7) and stirred at 500 rpm for 24 h at room temperature. The obtained dispersion was kept for dialysis to remove unbound NE [19,20].

The N-GQDs, NE-N-GQDs, GE11-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs were analyzed using zetasizer (Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, U.K.), UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV 3092, Shimadzu, Japan), and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (AXIS Supra, Shimadzu, Japan). The N-GQDs and GE11-N-GQDs were examined for change in fluorescence property using spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu RF-5301 PC, Japan). The surface morphology and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the GE11-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs were acquired using high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (Tecnai G2, F30).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of entrapment efficiency (%EE) and NE loading
ce:section.ce:para:
The NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs were dialyzed to remove free NE. The obtained samples were subjected to centrifugation for 30 min at 10,000 rpm (4°C) by centrifuge (C24 Plus, REMI, India). The amount of free NE was measured by recording absorbance of supernatant at 390 nm using UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV 3092, Shimadzu, Japan). The entrapment efficiency and NE loading were calculated as per the equations given below [19,21]:

where, Wtotal NE, Wfree NE and Wcarriers represent total weight of NE, weight of free NE and weight of carrier, respectively.

The solution of DPPH and dispersion of the NE, NE-N-GQDs, GE11-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs were mixed at 1:2 ratio. The reaction between DPPH and sample was performed in the dark for 1 h. The obtained solution mixture was analyzed for absorbance at 515 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV 3092, Shimadzu, Japan). Based on the absorbance value, antioxidant activity of the samples was measured using the equation given as follows [22]:

The erythrocytes or red blood cells (RBCs) were collected from the whole blood by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 20 min (4°C). The RBCs were washed with saline and dispersed. The RBCs dispersion were treated with the samples and incubated for 1 h (37°C). Then, the samples were centrifuged for 10 min at 3000 rpm (4°C) to collect supernatant and the absorbance recorded at 540 nm was used to calculate percentage erythrocytes hemolysis by the following equation [23]:

The release of NE suspension, NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs was performed at three different pH: phosphate buffer (PB) pH 5.5, 6.8 and 7.4 to mimic the tumor microenvironment and systemic circulation [24,25]. The dispersion of NE, NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs were poured in dialysis bag (MWCO between 12 and 14 kDa, Himedia) and kept in 200 ml of PB pH 5.5, 6.8 and 7.4 media at 37°C under continuous stirring (100 rpm). The aliquots (2 ml) were collected at predetermined time points and similar amount of fresh media was added to maintain sink condition. The samples were analyzed for the absorbance and % drug release was calculated [20].

Briefly, the A549 cells (a human lung adenocarcinoma cell line) were treated with different concentrations of the N-GQDs, NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs followed by incubation for 96 h. The MTT reagent [(3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide)] was mixed with the treated cells and kept for incubation for 4 h. After 4 h, the dark blue formazan product formed in the cells was dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and subjected for reading at 550 nm. The observed percentage inhibitions were plotted against the sample concentrations. The IC50 values were calculated [26].

Briefly, the A549 cells were treated with the N-GQDs and GE11-N-GQDs (30 µl/ml) for 4 h. Adhered cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (200 µl) thrice and fixed with 1 % formaldehyde. The cells were washed thrice with PBS and observed under fluorescence microscope after exposing samples to 365 nm laser [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cell cycle and apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: The A549 cells were treated with the NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs (10 µM) for 4 h. The cells were trypsinized and washed two times with PBS. The propidium iodide staining was performed and the samples were analyzed using a flow cytometer (BD FACS Calibur™) [26].

The NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs were stored at room temperature and refrigerated condition (2–8°C) for 6 months. The samples collected at an interval of one month were analyzed for particle size, zeta potential and %EE.

The experiments were conducted three times and the results are shown as mean ± standard deviation (S.D.). Statistical significance was calculated by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Significant differences were exhibited by p value < 0.05.

The NE was loaded on the surface of the N-GQDs by hydrophobic or π-π interaction between the aromatic regions of NE and N-GQDs [20], as shown in Fig. 2. Hence, the extent of NE loading depends on the hydrophobicity or the amount of delocalised π electrons available for hydrophobic interaction [27].

The schematic illustration of functionalization of the N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3. The activation of –COOH functional group of N-GQDs was attained by EDC, a water soluble carbodiimide which reacts with carboxyl group to form an O-acylisourea intermediate. This intermediate is highly reactive but less stable. Hence, NHS was used to stabilize by forming a stable NHS ester with an intermediate [28]. The NHS ester reacts with a primary amine of GE11 peptide and forms a stable amide bond and releases NHS which results in conjugation between the N-GQDs and GE11 peptide. The conjugation efficiency of GE11 peptide majorly depends on the concentration and ratio of EDC and NHS used for the reaction as they play a key role in the activation of –COOH functional groups and stabilization of an ester required for conjugation with primary amine. The conjugation efficiency can also be altered by the –COOH and –NH2 functional groups available for the reaction as well as the ratio of N-GQDs and GE11 peptide used during the process. The time required for activation of –COOH functional group is also considered as an important factor deciding the final product concentration after reaction. Hence, the stirring time needs to be optimized to increase the chances of amide conjugation.

The GE11-NE-N-GQDs were prepared by adsorption of NE on the surface of the GE11-N-GQDs (Fig. 3). The loading of NE is achieved by hydrophobic interaction and the hydrogen bond formation between the functional groups of NE and GE11 peptide [29].

The particle size of the N-GQDs, NE-N-GQDs, GE11-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs is displayed in Fig. 4A. The particle size of the NE-N-GQDs was found to be 96.88 ± 2.16 nm which is higher than the N-GQDs (79.59 ± 1.66 nm) ( Table 1). This is attributed to the adsorption of NE on the surface of N-GQDs. In addition, the particle size of GE11-N-GQDs increased to 103.5 ± 2.11 nm which is due to addition of large molecule (GE 11). The further increase in particle size of the GE11-NE-N-GQDs (124.0 ± 1.76 nm) is due to the adsorption of NE on the surface of GE11-N-GQDs.

This shows that the GE11-NE-N-GQDs can avoid rapid clearance by the reticuloendothelial system (RES) and are large enough to evade renal filtration. It also allows prolonged circulation time in the bloodstream and can enhance the bioavailability of drug. Since GE11 is attached, the particles can be easily captured by the cancer cells via receptor mediated endocytosis. Moreover, the particles can be cleared from body at a slow rate which offers prolonged drug release [30].

There was a slight shift in zeta potential of the N-GQDs from −11.34 ± 1.37 to −10.40 ± 1.45 mV after NE loading (Fig. S1A) (Table 1). This is because of reduced number of the –COO- functional group of N-GQDs. Additionally, the zeta potential value of the GE11-N-GQDs was changed to −7.27 ± 2.04 mV (Fig. S1B) which is attributed to the reduced number of surface –COO- group as –COOH group of N-GQDs are converted to amide form after covalent conjugation [29]. The further increase in zeta potential value with GE11-NE-N-GQDs (-3.87 ± 1.77 mV) (Fig. S1C) is due to loading of NE. Also, the formation of additional bond (e.g. hydrogen bond) between GE11-N-GQDs and NE reduces the availability of surface –COO- groups [19].

The zeta potential value is one of the factors that determines the interaction between nanomaterials and cancer cells. The negatively charged NE-N-GQDs can easily penetrate epithelial cells owing to the repulsive force between the NE-N-GQDs and mucus surface [31]. The negative charge also hinders rapid opsonisation of the NE-N-GQDs which enhances their stability in the blood stream and prolongs blood circulation time. Consequently, this can increase the absorption of NE at the target site [32]. For GE11-N-GQDs, the attachment of GE11 to EGFR via receptor-ligand interaction would increase cellular internalization [33]. Moreover, these particles can also be prevented from macrophage uptake and retained in systemic circulation for long period of time. This can maintain a stable pharmacokinetic profile. It also reduces the likelihood of aggregation in the bloodstream [34].

The change in electronic transition due to functionalization and NE loading can be clearly seen in Fig. 4B. The NE-N-GQDs show blue shift of a peak representing n to π* transition as compared to the N-GQDs due to formation of electron donor-acceptor complex [19]. Moreover, the presence of characteristic peaks of NE in the UV–visible spectrum of the NE-N-GQDs depicts successful loading of NE on the N-GQDs. The UV–visible spectrum of the GE11-N-GQDs against N-GQDs and GE11 is shown in fig. S2. The absorption peaks of the GE11 peptide are observed at 217 nm and 277 nm along with the absorption peaks of N-GQDs. The absorption peaks of N-GQDs and GE11 peptide are shifted and the maximum absorption is observed at 260 nm which indicate altered charge transfer due to the formation of an amide bond between the N-GQDs and GE11. The blue shift of the peak representing the amide linkage and the reduced intensity of characteristic peaks clearly indicate the loading of NE. This depicts the change in distribution of conjugated π system of the N-GQDs which can alter fluorescene property and drug loading capacity of the N-GQDs. This modification can also improve the drug delivery in cancer treatment [35].

The presence of sp2 hybridized CC aromatic regions in the structure of NE-N-GQDs is depicted by the C1s peak observed at 281.71 eV ( Fig. 5A). The intensity of C1s peak was reduced as compared to the C1s peak of N-GQDs [11] which indicates reduced amount of aromatic regions. The C1s peak observed at 284.84 eV is related to the C present in the 5 member ring of NE. The peak observed at 532.02 eV (O1s peak) is assigned to the surface –OH functional group of NE (Fig. 5B). The N1s peak displays the presence of –CN functional group of NE at 395.81 eV (Fig. 5C) [36]. The results confirm that the NE-N-GQDs are successfully prepared and the defect sites are increased.

The formation of amide linkage was confirmed by the N1s and O1s peaks at 396.74 eV, 398.80 eV and 528.79 eV, respectively (Fig. 5D and 5E). The N1s peaks represent N-H or iminic and –N-CO functional groups of the GE11 peptide. The O1s peak corresponding to amide carbonyl was also observed [37]. The GE11-NE-N-GQDs show characteristic peak of amide at 528.17 eV [38]. The characteristic XPS peaks of the N-GQDs and NE were also observed. However, the intensity of the C1s and N1s peaks was reduced as compared to the NE-N-GQDs (Fig. 5F, 5G and 5H). This demonstrates the reduction of sp2 hybridized aromatic regions and increased amount of defect with GE11 functionalization and NE loading which can facilitate wetting of the formulations and increase drug release.

A significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the GE11-N-GQDs (p < 0.05) can be seen in fig. S3. The fluorescence quenching is associated with an altered electron transition and electron density distribution which eventually influence the charge transfer or the electron donating ability of the N-GQDs. The red-shift of λem of the N-GQDs from 438 nm to 450 nm was also observed as the covalent functionalization modifies the chemistry of the N-GQDs and generates defect sites which increases non-radiative electron-hole pair recombination and shifts λem to the longer wavelength region [39]. The other factor contributing to the red-shift is increased particle size distribution [40]. The fluorescence property of the N-GQDs based nanocarriers can be utilized for bioimaging and drug delivery monitoring. The fluorescence quenching of the GE11-N-GQDs can be further used as an indicator for fluorescence based sensing of biomolecules [41].

The HR-TEM images exhibit effect of GE11 functionalization and NE loading on the morphological characteristic of the N-GQDs based formulation. The GE11 functionalization can provide more uniform and stable particles. It forms a layer around the surface of the N-GQDs and increases thickness of the N-GQDs which can eventually increase the particle size [42]. The GE11-N-GQDs show spherical and uniformly distributed particles in the range of 40–110 nm and maximum number of particles are of 85.05 ± 3.40 nm ( Fig. 6A). The SAED pattern depicts small, randomly distributed bright spots along with the diffused rings (Fig. 6B) as opposed to the bright spots in symmetric hexagonal pattern depicted by the N-GQDs in our previous research [11]. This demonstrates an altered crystalline nature of the N-GQDs is due to GE11 functionalization. The d spacing value was found to be 0.31 ± 0.18 nm which is higher than the d spacing value of the N-GQDs. This indicates that the distance between graphene planes is increased owing the addition of GE11 peptide. The loading of NE can also affect the arrangement or shape of the particles where the spherical structure can be changed to more defined structures [43]. Hence, the GE11-NE-N-GQDs exhibit cubic and quasi-hexagonal particles (Fig. 6C). These structures can increase the drug loading and facilitate control release [44]. The mean particle size was observed as 95.04 ± 2.65 nm with particles distributed in the range of 60–120 nm. The corresponding SAED pattern represents reduced crystallinity by diffused rings (Fig. 6D). The d spacing value was further increased to 0.45 ± 0.22 nm as the NE was present on the surface of GE11-N-GQDs. The increased defect indicates diminished crystallinity and structural integrity. This can increase the interaction of the GE11-NE-N-GQDs with aqueous media which may lead to high dispersion stability and drug release.

The %EE of NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs was estimated to be 92.36 ± 1.68 % and 98.14 ± 1.32 %, respectively (Table 1). This indicates that the GE11-N-GQDs are more capable to retain high amount of NE and prevent the loss of NE during drug delivery.

The direct correlation between the amount of NE added in N-GQDs and GE11-N-GQDs and %loading is observed in the graph ( Fig. 7A). The high drug loading (97.38 ± 1.46 %) was observed at N-GQDs:NE ratio of 1:9. This is attributed to the π-π stacking or hydrophobic interaction between NE and N-GQDs [45]. The hydrophobic interaction is increased with the increased availability of aromatic regions which is associated with the amount of hydrophobic drugs. The increased quantity of hydrophobic material provides additional aromatic regions for hydrophobic interactions which led to enhanced loading of NE. In comparison to the NE-N-GQDs, the GE11-NE-N-GQDs show increased NE loading at GE11-N-GQDs:NE ratio of 1:7 with the observed value of 97.42 ± 1.15 %. The high EE and NE loading with GE11-N-GQDs are due to the formation of additional hydrogen bond between the functional group of NE and GE11 peptide apart from hydrophobic π- π stacking [29]. The high NE loading ensures greater availability of NE at the target site which will lead to effective drug delivery and minimize toxicity. It ultimately could increase the anticancer effect of NE which could reduce the dose and dosing frequency.

The color change of the solution mixtures to yellow indicates the samples have antioxidant activity (Fig. 7B). The color intensity of the solution depends on the structural and chemical properties of the samples which can be seen with the values obtained from the calculation (Fig. 7C and Table 1). The antioxidant activity of NE is ascribed to the sp2 hybridized aromatic regions, N atoms and –CH3 groups of NE. These functional groups donate electrons to the DPPH free radical and neutralize the activity of DPPH free radical [46]. The antioxidant activity of NE-N-GQDs was reduced as compared to the N-GQDs which is attributed to the increased defect sites due to NE loading. It influences the electron donating ability of the functional groups present in NE-N-GQDs and reduces the interaction between NE-N-GQDs and DPPH free radical. Compared to the N-GQDs, the antioxidant activity of GE11-N-GQDs was diminished due to the dissociation of delocalized electron and decreased sp2 hybridization [47]. Furthermore, new defect sites are introduced in GE11-NE-N-GQDs, affecting the interaction between the GE11-NE-N-GQDs and free radical.

The values also exhibit that the formulations possess > 50 % efficiency in neutralization of free radical like reactive oxygen species (ROS) which can increase drug delivery efficiency as the formulation can be prevented from oxidative degradation [48]. Moreover, free radical neutralization property of the N-GQDs based formulations can be used to protect healthy cells from oxidative damage since free radicals have been associated with the risk of cancer. The antioxidant property reduces side effect in healthy cells by neutralizing unstable free radicals which can cause cellular damage. The antioxidants donate electron to free radicals for neutralization and prevent damage of DNA, proteins and cell membrane. The cancer cells often produce high levels of free radicals which increase oxidative stress. The antioxidant property maintain a balance by reducing these free radical levels and protect cells from oxidative damage which may also have induced by cancer treatments like chemotherapy and radiation. Hence, the increased oxidative stress in healthy cells can be reduced by these formulations which can prevent cancer cell growth [49]. It is reported that the antioxidants can also inhibit cancer cell growth by acting as ‘pro-oxidants’. The redox control is impaired in cancer cells which leads to production of high level of free radicals as compared to healthy cells. Pro-oxidants increase oxidative stress in cancer cells which can trigger cell death pathways to selectively target and kill cancer cells. When administered in calibrated dose, the same level of oxidative stress does not reach the apoptotic threshold in normal cells. Therefore, controlled enhancement of intracellular production of free radical like ROS using pro-oxidative substance is crucial in cancer treatment. Hence, the N-GQDs based formulations with antioxidant activity can induce cancer cell death using the abnormal redox condition of cancer cells [50,51].

The calculated erythrocytes toxicity induced by the NE-N-GQDs, GE11-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs was 2.86 ± 0.36 %, 0.94 ± 0.34 % and 1.50 ± 0.42 %, respectively (Table 1). The value shows that these N-GQDs based nanomaterials are hemo-compatible and are suitable for the various biomedical applications such as bioimaging and systemic drug delivery, as per the ASTM standard (ASTM E2524–08) (<5 %). The results are supported by the photographs which reveal an absolute contrast in color of the supernatant (Fig. 7D). The red supernatant indicates the RBCs rupture induced by triton X-100, while the clear, colorless supernatant suggests low hemolytic activity of the samples.

It is imperative to understand the fate of anticancer drug in complex tumor environment to increase the effect of drug. To achieve therapeutic effects, drug must be delivered and released into specific cellular compartments, typically intracellular regions such as cytoplasm or nucleus of target cells. The intracellular environment responsive nanosystems can maintain good extracellular stability while rapidly releasing drugs inside cancer cells. The increased release in intracellular region is considered as a great approach for targeted anticancer drug delivery as it releases drug directly at the site of action (intracellular region). It depicts that the release in intracellular region is more beneficial for targeted drug release and to offer effective drug delivery [52]. Most often intracellular pH of tumor enhances the release of pH sensitive drugs which makes a drug-carrier system more targeted and effective to deliver drug at the tumor site. Thus, the N-GQDs based nanocarrier was examined for drug release at different pH conditions. The in vitro drug release profiles of NE suspension, NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs in PB pH 5.5, pH 6.8 and pH 7.4 comprising 0.5 % tween 80 are shown in Fig. 8. These profiles represent release at intracellular region, extracellular region of cancer cells and systemic circulation.

It can be seen that the release of NE suspension is relatively low as compared to the NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs (Fig. 8A). The precipitates were observed in the dialysis bag after 8 h. This is ascribed to the low solubility of the NE [53]. The NE-N-GQDs show 96.99 ± 2.36 % release at 36 h with high release rate as compared to the GE11-NE-N-GQDs (Table 1). The increased release of NE-N-GQDs is associated with small particle size in comparison to the GE11-NE-N-GQDs and increased wetting of the particles on the account of the formation of defect sites [54]. The GE11-N-GQDs show the prolonged release (99.78 ± 2.20 % at 50 h) with slow release rate which is ascribed to the hydrogen bond formed between the GE11 peptide and NE which can hold NE for long period of time and prevent fast release of NE [29]. In this case, the release rate can be easily modulated by altering the concentration of GE11 peptide as it is linked with the thickness of layer formed around N-GQDs during amide comjugation. The thick layer can delay the diffusion of NE from the nanocarrier. Moreover, the more amount of GE11 attached on the surface can introduce more number of sites or functional group available for hydrogen bond formation with NE which could contribute to more prolonged release.

It was observed that the release rate of the NE suspension, NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs is pH dependent as the solubility of NE is significantly decreased at pH> 3 (Fig. 8B, 8C and 8D) [53]. The release rate of NE suspension, NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs was observed in the order pH 5.5 >pH 6.8 >pH 7.4. It is also presumably attributed to the protonation of NE at an intracellular pH which weakens the hydrophobic interaction and increases release of NE. The hydrogen bond is stronger under normal physiological condition than intracellular region which causes low NE release from the N-GQDs (52.66 ± 1.96 % vs. 23.43 ± 2.85 %) at 24 h. The similar pattern was exhibited by the GE11-NE-N-GQDs. This pH dependent release profile can be majorly influenced by the interaction between GE11-N-GQDs and NE which is stronger at the physiological pH than at intracellular pH due to the degradation of NE at low pH condition. The protonation of functional group of NE at an intracellular pH hampers the structure require to form stable and strong hydrogen bond, ultimately increasing release of NE in intracellular region of tumor. This highlights the importance of hydrogen bond interaction between the NE and GE11-N-GQDs in modification of release profile.

Considering the high release rate of the formulations at intracellular pH, it is expected that NE can be delivered to target region (intracellular region) of cancer cells. This facilitates the uptake and retention of NE which increase the interaction of NE with the intracellular components like mitochondria and hampers the function of cancer cells. This increases the effect of NE in killing cancer cells. Since NE is released more at the intracellular region of cancer cells and less at the systemic pH of healthy cells, there is less possibility of affecting normal cells by reducing the side effects asscoiated with NE. Moreover, this pH sensitive release can help to bypass the resistance mechanism as the drug exerts its therapeutic effect only at the target site [55]. The release profiles also depicted that the NE is stable at neutral systemic condition. It can help to maintain the therapeutic concentration of NE for longer period of time as the hydrogen bond tends to remain stable at physiological condition which prevents degradation and release of NE at the normal physiological condition

The release profiles also highlight the benefit of the N-GQDs based formulations in comparison to other nanocarriers in order to extend or modify the release according to the requirement and maintain therapeutic concentration of drug in tumor cells by prolonged release. For instance, various nanocarrier systems including polymeric nanoparticles, inorganic nanoparticles, liposomes, mesoporous silica nanoparticles and graphene nanosheets exhibited high release rate at an intracellular pH in comparison to physiological pH but burst release and more extended drug release were observed which can increase the possibility of toxicity and fluctuation in drug concentration [56–60].

The concentration dependent viability of A549 cells is observed ( Fig. 9A). The N-GQDs show cell viability > 75 % at all concentration and 50 % of the cells were inhibited (IC50) at a concentrations > 10 µg/ml. This exhibits that N-GQDs have no cytotoxic effect on A549 cells and are suitable nanocarrier for drug delivery. There was a significant difference in the viability of the cells treated with the NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs when compared with the N-GQDs. The NE-N-GQDs showed higher cell inhibition than the N-GQDs with the IC50 value 3 µg/ml which is lower than the reported value of free NE (6.60 µg/ml) [61]. The further reduction in the IC50 value was observed with the GE11-NE-N-GQDs (1 µg/ml) (Table 1). This indicates that the low concentration of the NE-N-GQDs is sufficient to induce more cytotoxic effect in comparison to the free NE. The increased potency of the GE11-N-GQDs is ascribed to high affinity of the GE11 peptide to EGFR which could effectively target EGFR and increase the accumulation of the NE in A549 cells as compared to the NE-N-GQDs [62]. The accumulation of NE due to the NE-N-GQDs in A549 cells is due to the small size, high NE loading and high drug release rate which ensures that high amount of NE is achieved at the target site to kill cancer cells. The increased solubility of NE due to the loading on the N-GQDs and GE11-N-GQDs can be considered another reason for increased cytotoxicity. With the high amount of defect produced in the GE11-NE-N-GQDs, high solubilization of NE is anticipated in comparison to the NE-N-GQDs. The increased cytotoxicity of GE11-NE-N-GQDs can be defined in the following order: (i) the GE11-NE-N-GQDs target EGFR and internalize in the cells, (ii) the high intracellular accumulation increases the availability of the GE11-NE-N-GQDs to induce cytotoxic effect, (iii) the increased surface area of the formulation due to GE11 functionalization increases the active sites available for the interaction with the cells which triggers various inflammatory responses, damages cell membrane and interacts with genetic material to induce cytotoxicity [63].

The inherent blue fluorescence of the N-GQDs and GE11-N-GQDs are used for imaging of the A549 cells rather than using the organic dyes. Thus, these nanocarriers are promising alternative to organic dyes in imaging of the cancer cells. The A549 cells depict blue fluorescence of the N-GQDs and GE11-N-GQDs (Fig. 9B and 9C). This shows that the nanocarriers are internalized into the cells and able to deliver drug at the target site. However, the intensity of the blue fluorescence was higher in GE11-N-GQDs treated cells than the N-GQDs treated cells. It depicts that the GE11-N-GQDs could bind to EGFR overexpressed A549 cells and increase intracellular accumulation as compared to the N-GQDs [64].

The surface negative charge of N-GQDs and GE11-N-GQDs assists their penetration into the cells [65]. In contrast to the N-GQDs, GE11 functionalization promotes the cell internalization by receptor-mediated uptake of the GE11-N-GQDs. The receptor-ligand complex of GE11 and EGFR allows more accumulation which increases interaction between the GE11-N-GQDs and cellular components. It depicts that the high amount of drug can be delivered to the target site (intracellular region), released at endosomal pH and provide anticancer effect. Moreover, the GE11 binding inhibits cell proliferation by blocking the signaling pathway of EGFR which is also responsible to increase cell cytotoxicity [66]. It indicates that the GE11-N-GQDs could inhibit phosphorylation of phosphoinositid (PI3K) and protein kinase B (AKT) associated with EGFR signaling, which is responsible for cancer cell proliferation. The other possible pathways involved with EGFR signaling such as Ras/Raf/MEK/ERK are responsible to regulate gene expression by altering signals from cell surface receptors, could also be affected. Therefore, EGFR targeting by GE11 peptide can increase the therapeutic potential of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cell cycle and apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: The cell cycle is determined by the various events that lead to cell division in normal cells. The change in normal function of cell cycle can induce cancer and hence, it is important to identify the phase due to which uncontrolled growth is occurred. The cell cycle arrest by the formulation determines the mechanism of inhibition of cancer cell proliferation and progression which is an important aspect to display effective clinical application of the formulation. Hence, the NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs were studied for their effect on cancer cell progression through cell cycle arrest. The cell growth and division comprise four phases: (i) Gap1 or G1 phase during which cell growth occurs, (ii) Synthesis or S phase at which DNA replication can be observed, (iii) Gap2 or G2 phase where cells prepare for mitosis and (iv) mitosis, M phase where two daughter cells are formed. The graph of the untreated, N-GQDs and GE11-N-GQDs treated A549 cells showed G1, S, G2 and sub-G1 phase (Fig. 9D). The cell count in G1 phase was reduced by 50 % in GE11-NE-N-GQDs treated cells when compared with untreated cells. This observation shows that the combination of GE11 functionalization and NE loading effectively inhibits cancer cell growth since GE11 and NE can bind to EGFR. The EGFR inhibiton can impair the recruitment of DNA replication proteins to the replication forks. This can result in replication errors and DNA damage. This leads to arrest cells in the G1 phase and prevents cells from progressing to the S phase where DNA replication occurs. Hence, the S phase showed less number of cells after treatment with the NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs which exhibits that the formulations are able to halt DNA replication. There was a considerable reduction in cell count in G2 phase with the GE11-NE-N-GQDs treated cells which shows that the cells are prohibited from mitosis and eventually cell division is stopped. Moreover, the apoptosis was found to be increased with GE11-NE-N-GQDs (67.66 %) as compared to the NE-N-GQDs (31.75 %) which can be seen from the increased cell count in sub-G1 phase. It supports the results of cytotoxicity study. The apoptosis in NE-N-GQDs treated A549 cells is attributed to the reduced tyrosine kinase activity by NE which inhibits cell proliferation, survival and angiogenesis. The NE also affects the epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and prevents spread of cancer cells [67]. The further rise in the apoptosis with GE11-NE-N-GQDs is ascribed to the combined inhibitory effect of GE11 and NE. The binding of GE11-NE-N-GQDs inhibits EGFR signaling pathway such as p27 induced inhibition of cyclin dependent kinase activity which eventually affects the cell cycle and inhibits cell proliferation [68]. Additionally, the increased cellular uptake due to GE11 peptide increases the interaction with mitochondria and hampers the function of mitochondria. This induces the apoptosis by inducing mitochondrial free radicals, considering their potential as pro-oxidants. Apart from signaling pathway, the targeted drug release also contribute to the observed apoptosis [62]. The combination of all these factors prevents cancer cell proliferation and metastasis which increases drug delivery efficiency of NE using GE11-N-GQDs as a drug nanocarrier.

The stability of the NE-N-GQDs and GE11-NE-N-GQDs stored for 6 months at refrigerated condition and room temperature was determined using particle size, zeta potential and %EE. The particle size and zeta potential of NE-N-GQDs stored at refrigerated condition were not largely affected for 4 months. After 4 months, the considerable change in particle size was observed ( Fig. 10A) which is indicative of altered properties of NE-N-GQDs after 4 months. The zeta potential and %EE of the NE-N-GQDs were also observed to be changed after 4 months (Fig. 10B and 10C). Contrary, there was a negligible change in particle size, zeta potential and %EE observed with GE11-NE-N-GQDs for 6 months. This indicates that GE11 functionalization increases stability of the formulation as the presence of GE11 peptide prevents aggregation of the formulation by steric hindrance. The steric barrier of the GE11 can also reduce the rapid clearance of the formulation from the systemic circulation and increase the absorption of NE [69]. However, the large aggregates were observed and substantial change in the particle size and zeta potential can be seen in the formulations after completion of one month at room temperature (Fig. 10D and 10E) which indicates the instability of the formulations at room temperature. The %EE of GE11-NE-N-GQDs was not largely affected at refrigerated condition which shows that the amount of NE remained unchanged for 6 months when GE11-NE-N-GQDs were stored at 2–8°C. The formulations showed variation in %EE at room tempeature (Fig. 10F). Overall, the study demostrates refrigerated condition as a suitable storage condition of the GE11-NE-N-GQDs.

The present study focuses on the development N-GQDs based formulation for the targeted delivery of NE to increase the efficiency for the treatment of NSCLC. The N-GQDs were modified to target EGFR overexpressed lung cancer cells by GE11 functionalization. The NE was successfully loaded on N-GQDs and GE11-N-GQDs. It is clearly evident that the hydrophobicity and hydrogen bonding increase NE loading. The GE11-N-GQDs increases intracellular internalization and the potency to kill A549 cancer cells. The GE11-NE-N-GQDs increase apoptosis and inhibit EGFR related cell proliferation and metastasis. Overall, the study exhibits that the GE11-NE-N-GQDs are suitable nanocarrier for EGFR targeted bioimaging and treatment of NSCLC. With the consideration of promising in vitro results, the present research would be directed towards in vivo studies such as pharmacokinetics, biodistribution, tumor accumulation and toxicity, in order to translate it for clinical applications. This would help in exploring the application of the N-GQDs to study the therapeutic effect and biocompatibility when other target moieties are used. It would also be beneficial in optimization of drug loading and applicability of the N-GQDs in targeted delivery of other anticancer drugs. The unique and advantageous fluorescence property of the N-GQDs hold significant potential in multimodal imaging which could increase the efficiency in clinical diagnosis of cancer.
Kansara Vrushti: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Patel Mitali: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.
